narrative_blocks.header: Sick at Fort Edward in 1756
narrative_blocks.body: He served in Capt. Lacey's Company (Fourth Regiment, Col. Andrew Ward Jr.) and appears in the Oct 13, 1756 muster roll as "sick at Fort Edward" during the Crown Point campaign. The following year he rode with Capt. Smedley's relief company for 16 days following the French and Indian massacre at Fort William Henry in August 1757.

narrative_blocks.header: "A mechanic of competent fortune"
narrative_blocks.body: He is described as "a mechanic of competent fortune" whose wife Mary "were very pious." He served as Ensign in the Farmington militia (October 1750) and rose to Captain by October 1752. He gave his son John one week to decide about college — an offer John accepted before the week expired. John later became the 21st Governor of Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: His 1781 will was witnessed by Col. Noadiah
narrative_blocks.body: His will, dated December 18, 1781, was witnessed by three prominent Farmington neighbors: Col. Noadiah Hooker, Martin Bull (, the goldsmith and engraver), and Rebekah Hooker. He appointed his wife Mary as executor and his son John as co-executor — the same John who would become Governor. He died on January 11, 1782, just weeks after signing.

The following year he rode with Capt. Smedley's relief company for 16 days following the French and Indian massacre at Fort William Henry in August 1757.

military_service.unit: Captain Lacey's Company, Fourth Regiment (Col. Andrew Ward Jr.); later Capt. Smedley's Company

battles.notes: Fort William Henry, Oct 13, 1756 — sick at Fort Edward
Relief of Fort William Henry, Aug 1757 — 16 days service with Capt. Smedley's Company

narrative_blocks.header: Named executor of her husband's 1781 will
narrative_blocks.body: She and Ephraim "were very pious." When Ephraim signed his will in December 1781, he appointed her as primary executor alongside their son John — who would become Governor of Connecticut seventeen years later. She outlived her husband by sixteen years, dying December 26, 1798 at approximately age 85.

institutions.institution_blurb: Member, American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions.

cross_connections.display_label: the Hooker descendant who married his daughter Dorothy

narrative_blocks.header: His father gave him one week to decide
narrative_blocks.body: His father Ephraim — a stern Puritan militia captain — told him at sixteen that he had one week to decide whether to receive a college education. John accepted before the week expired. He prepared under the Rev. Timothy Pitkin, then entered Yale in 1763.

narrative_blocks.header: Assumed the governorship when the sitting governor died
narrative_blocks.body: He served as Lieutenant Governor for eleven years under Gov. Jonathan Trumbull Jr. On August 7, 1809, Trumbull died in office, and Treadwell assumed the governorship. He was elected in his own right in 1810, receiving 10,265 votes to 7,185 for the Republican candidate.

narrative_blocks.header: "Father of common school education"
narrative_blocks.body: He negotiated the $1,200,000 sale of northeastern Ohio land to endow the Connecticut School Fund — the permanent foundation for public education in the state. He was appointed to the Board of Managers to administer the Fund and is remembered as "the father of common school education." He also co-founded the Connecticut Academy of Arts and Sciences (incorporated 1799).

narrative_blocks.header: The last Puritan governor
narrative_blocks.body: He is considered the last Puritan governor of Connecticut — in him, wrote his obituary, "we see blended for the last time the theologian and statesman." He was a founding member and President (1798-1822) of the Connecticut Missionary Society, the oldest missionary society in the United States, sending Congregational ministers to new settlements in Vermont, New Hampshire, and western New York.

narrative_blocks.header: Failed as a merchant — recovered by manufacturing nitre
narrative_blocks.body: After Yale he rejected a law career to work the family farm, then ventured into mercantile trade and failed badly. He recovered by manufacturing nitre (saltpetre), extricating himself from the financial loss. He went on to serve as Farmington's Probate Judge for 26 years, which he treasured for the chance to help widows, orphans, and the less fortunate through wills and estates.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Hooker men married into the Treadwell family
narrative_blocks.body: EH called Mary Treadwell the "daughter" of Gov. John Treadwell — but Treadwell was born in 1745 and she in 1755; he was ten at her birth. Ephraim Treadwell's 1781 will confirms both as siblings.

artworks.artwork_blurb: Portrait by George F. Wright, Museum of Connecticut History.

burial.plot_notes: Buried next to husband John Treadwell.

narrative_blocks.header: She was the Governor's sister
narrative_blocks.body: EH entry 306 called her "daughter of Gov. John Treadwell" — but Treadwell was born in 1745, making him ten years old at her 1755 birth. Both were children of Ephraim Treadwell, confirmed by his 1781 will naming "daughter Mary" and "son John Treadwell" as executor.

burial.plot_notes: Saint James Episcopal Church Memorial Garden, Farmington CT

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Georgian Colonial; family home for three Hooker generations.

cross_connections.display_label: First cousins who served together — Roger enlisted in Noadiah's company.
First cousins once removed — both married into the Treadwell family.

notable.notable_blurb: Continental Army Major; Society of the Cincinnati

institutions.institution_blurb: Member of the Society of the Cincinnati — Revolutionary War officers' hereditary society.

narrative_blocks.header: First from Farmington to Boston — Noadiah led
narrative_blocks.body: At the Lexington alarm he volunteered in the company of his first cousin Capt. Noadiah Hooker — 111 men, the first company from Farmington and the first from Connecticut to reach the Continental Army at Boston. He entered as a private and rose through sergeant, ensign, and 2nd lieutenant to Major of the Continental Line.

narrative_blocks.header: Flag of truce — Burgoyne's surrender delivered
narrative_blocks.body: He was sent under a flag of truce to the British commander in New York to formally announce the surrender of Burgoyne's army in October 1777. He also helped fit out fire ships at New York in 1776 under Washington's plan to attack British vessels. EH records he was "throughout the war held in high esteem by Gen. Washington, and frequently entrusted with responsible and important duties."

narrative_blocks.header: West Indies mariner — then Sheriff until 70
narrative_blocks.body: Before the Revolution he made eleven voyages to the West Indies as a merchant mariner. After the war he became Sheriff of Hartford County, served as Justice of the Peace until age 70, and was a Farmington selectman. He disposed of his estate to his brother-in-law before his death, retaining possession during his life.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife was the Governor's sister, not daughter
narrative_blocks.body: EH called Mary Treadwell the "daughter" of Gov. John Treadwell — impossible, as Treadwell was born 1745 and Mary in 1755, making him only ten at her birth. Ephraim Treadwell's 1781 will names both "daughter Mary" and "son John Treadwell" as executor, confirming they were siblings.

narrative_blocks.header: Purchased Tunxis land with Elnathan in 1776
narrative_blocks.body: In 1776, he and his brother Elnathan purchased 40 acres at Congee, Farmington — including a dwelling house and barn — from Tunxis men Cornelius Indian and Elijah Wampey for £150. The deed is preserved at the Native Northeast Portal as one of the documented transfers of Native land in the Farmington area.

narrative_blocks.header: Cincinnati member — half-brother Thomas died in war
narrative_blocks.body: He and his brother-in-law Capt. Simeon Newell were both original members of the Society of the Cincinnati, founded 1783 by Continental officers. His half-brother Thomas Hart Hooker (different mother, Mercy Hart) died of pleurisy during the same Revolution — at Roxbury in 1775, age 30 — never seeing its end.

military_service.unit: Company of Capt. Noadiah Hooker; Continental Line (eventually Major)
military_service.notes: Enlisted at first call (Lexington alarm) in company of his cousin Capt. Noadiah Hooker — 111 men, first from Farmington and first from Connecticut to reach the army at Boston. Rose: Private → Sergeant → Ensign → 2nd Lieutenant (under Washington) → Major. 1776: Fitted out fire ships at New York to attack British vessels (Washington's plan). As brigade major: managed correspondence for generals on whose staff he served. Sent under flag of truce to British commander in New York to announce Burgoyne's surrender. "Throughout the war held in high esteem by Gen. Washington, and frequently entrusted with responsible and important duties." Member, Society of the Cincinnati.

burialCemetery.name: Memento Mori Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Rev. Samuel Hooker buried here; no stone survives.
